questions.question: Tell me about yourself
questions.answer:
Structure your answer as: Present → Past → Future (2-3 minutes max).

Template:

"I'm a .NET developer with about 5 years of experience, currently [role/looking for]. I work primarily with ASP.NET Core, Angular, and SQL Server — building web applications from the API layer all the way to the frontend.

Before that, I worked on [briefly mention key projects/responsibilities]. One of the projects I'm most proud of is [FolioTrack/offline invoice/etc.] where I built [specific feature] that [specific impact].

I'm now looking for [type of role] where I can [growth goal — work on larger scale systems / deepen expertise in cloud / lead technically]. I'm particularly interested in this role because [specific reason about the company/tech stack]."

Key principles:

  • Keep it professional — no personal life details unless relevant
  • Lead with current state, not your whole career history
  • Mention 1-2 specific achievements (with numbers if possible)
  • End with why you're here — connects you to this company
  • Practice until it's smooth — don't read from a script but don't ramble

followUps.question: Why are you looking to change jobs?
followUps.answer:
Be honest but professional. Frame it around what you're moving toward, not what you're running from. Avoid badmouthing your current employer.

Good answers:

  • "I've grown a lot in my current role and I'm ready for a bigger challenge — specifically working on [higher-scale systems / more complex domains / larger teams]."
  • "The company's direction shifted away from the technical work I'm most passionate about. I want to stay on the engineering track rather than moving toward [consultancy / maintenance mode]."
  • "I've been with the same company for [X] years and I think it's time to broaden my perspective by seeing how other teams approach problems."
  • "I'm specifically excited about [this company/role] because [genuine reason]. That kind of opportunity isn't available in my current position."

Avoid:

  • "My manager is terrible" — even if true
  • "The pay is bad" — even if true (mention compensation as one factor if pressed, but never lead with it)
  • "I'm bored" — say instead "I'm looking for more challenge"

followUps.question: What are you looking for in your next role?
followUps.answer:
Be specific. Generic answers ("good culture," "growth opportunities") sound like you haven't thought about it. Anchor your answer to real, verifiable things you value.

Good structure:

  • Technical: "I want to work on systems where [scale / complexity / domain] pushes me technically. Specifically, I'm looking to deepen my skills in [cloud infrastructure / distributed systems / performance engineering]."
  • Collaborative: "I do my best work when I'm on a team where [code review culture is strong / engineers own their features end-to-end / there's room to suggest architectural ideas]."
  • Product/impact: "I want to work on something that [ships to real users / has measurable impact / solves a problem I care about]."
  • Growth: "I want a role where there's a clear path to [senior / tech lead / architect] and where the company invests in that growth."

End by tying it back: "From what I've seen about this role, [specific thing about the job/team] seems to align well with that."

questions.tip: Customize the ending for each company. Research the tech stack, team size, product, and mention specifically why this company/role aligns with your goals. Generic "I want to grow" endings are forgettable.

questions.question: Tell me about a challenging project you worked on and how you handled it
questions.answer:
Use the STAR framework: Situation → Task → Action → Result.

Example answer (PaymentReminder project):

Situation: We had a PaymentReminder background service in .NET 8 that was supposed to send reminder emails to clients with overdue invoices. The service worked fine in dev but in production it was occasionally sending duplicate reminders to the same client — causing client complaints.

Task: I needed to identify why duplicates were happening and fix it without disrupting the service (it ran every hour).

Action: I added structured logging (Serilog) to trace each email send with the invoice ID and timestamp. The logs revealed the issue: when two server instances ran simultaneously (after a scale-out), both would pick up the same invoices from the DB because there was no distributed lock. I implemented an idempotency approach — marking invoices as "reminder-sent" in a DB update within the same transaction as the email send. I also added a unique constraint on the reminder tracking table so duplicate records were impossible at the DB level.

Result: Zero duplicate reminders in the 4 months since the fix. The extra logging also helped us catch other issues faster.

followUps.question: What did you learn from it?
followUps.answer:
Give a genuine, specific takeaway — not a generic "I learned the importance of testing." The best answers show you changed how you work as a result.

Example:

"The biggest thing I took away was that distributed systems have failure modes that local testing simply can't reproduce. Two processes picking up the same record is obvious in retrospect, but it never showed up in dev because we always ran one instance. Now, whenever I design a background service or scheduled job, I start by asking: what happens if two instances of this run simultaneously? Idempotency is no longer an afterthought — it's a design requirement.

I also learned the value of structured logging as an early investment. The debugging would have taken days without it. Now I add correlation IDs and operation context to every background service before it goes to production."

Format: State the lesson → explain how it changed your behavior → mention at least one thing you now do differently.

followUps.question: What would you do differently?
followUps.answer:
Show reflection without being too hard on yourself. The goal is demonstrating maturity and continuous improvement.

Example:

"If I were starting that project today, I would have designed for idempotency from day one rather than retrofitting it. When I first built the service, I knew we might scale to multiple instances eventually, but I deferred it as a 'later problem.' That was the wrong call — the cost of adding a distributed lock or idempotency key during initial design is maybe half a day. Retrofitting it safely in production was three days of careful work and coordination.

I'd also write an integration test that spins up two instances of the service against a shared in-memory database to simulate the concurrency scenario. That test didn't exist because the failure mode wasn't obvious — now I know to look for it."

Structure: One concrete thing you'd do earlier/differently → why it would have helped → what the cost/benefit difference would have been.

questions.tip: STAR answers should be specific, not generic. "We had a bug, I fixed it" is not a story. The interviewer wants to understand your problem-solving process, how you communicate under pressure, and how you handle ambiguity.

questions.question: Tell me about a conflict you had with a team member and how you resolved it
questions.answer:
Use STAR. Show maturity — you focused on the problem, not the person.

Key principles for conflict questions:

  • Never blame the other person — "they were wrong, I was right" is a red flag
  • Show you took initiative to resolve it, didn't wait for a manager
  • Focus on the outcome and what you both learned
  • Demonstrate empathy — you understood their perspective

Example structure:

"In [project], I disagreed with a colleague about [technical/process decision]. They wanted to [their approach], while I believed [your approach] was better because [technical reason].

Instead of escalating immediately, I set up a short one-on-one to understand their reasoning. It turned out they had [context I didn't know about — deadline pressure, constraint]. Once I understood their concern, we agreed to [compromise or you adjusted your view].

The outcome was [result]. I learned that [what you took away — importance of understanding context before disagreeing, how to frame technical disagreements as options with tradeoffs rather than right/wrong]."

followUps.question: How do you handle disagreements about technical decisions with senior developers?
followUps.answer:
Show that you can advocate confidently without being arrogant or dismissive of experience.

Good approach:

"When I disagree with a senior developer's technical direction, I first make sure I fully understand their reasoning — sometimes what looks like a suboptimal decision makes sense given constraints I wasn't aware of (legacy system behavior, team capability, delivery timeline).

If I still have a concern after understanding their reasoning, I raise it clearly but frame it as a question or an option rather than a contradiction: 'I see why this approach works — I want to flag one concern about [X] and see if it changes your thinking.' I'll often prepare a lightweight comparison: the proposed approach vs my alternative, with the trade-offs laid out honestly.

If they've considered my point and still prefer their approach, I execute it fully and professionally. The senior engineer's experience is real, and disagreement doesn't mean my view is always right. What I won't do is implement their decision half-heartedly or make passive-aggressive comments to the team — that's worse than the technical choice itself."

followUps.question: What do you do when you strongly disagree with a decision but are outvoted?
followUps.answer:
The phrase interviewers want to hear: "disagree and commit."

"If I've clearly stated my concern, the team has heard it, and we've decided to go in a different direction — I commit fully to executing that decision. Partial buy-in is more dangerous than the technical disagreement itself: if I half-implement something or signal to the team that I think it's wrong, I undermine the whole team's ability to execute.

That said, I'd document my concern — a brief note in the design doc, ticket, or PR description: 'We considered [alternative approach] and chose [this approach] because [reason]. Concern flagged: [risk].'. That way, if the risk materializes later, the team learns from it rather than just experiencing it as a surprise.

I also try to separate 'I disagree with this decision' from 'this person has bad judgment.' Smart engineers disagree all the time, and being wrong occasionally is normal. What matters is whether the team learns from it and whether my concern was genuinely heard."

questions.tip: "Disagree and commit" is a key phrase. If you voiced your concern clearly and the team decided otherwise, you commit fully to executing the decision. Passive resistance ("fine, we'll do it your way") is a red flag. Show you can advocate for your view and then move forward.

questions.question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know: Are you serious about growing? Will you stay long enough to be worth hiring? Are your goals realistic?

Good answer structure:

"In 5 years, I see myself as a senior or lead .NET developer — someone who's not just writing code but helping define architecture decisions, mentoring junior developers, and having a technical influence on the product direction.

Short-term, in the next 1-2 years, I want to deepen my expertise in [cloud-native development / microservices / system design] and contribute to more complex, higher-scale systems than I've worked on so far.

Longer-term, I'm open to either a technical track (principal engineer, architect) or a hybrid technical lead role — I want to stay hands-on with code but also have more impact on the system design and team direction. This role seems like a great step toward that because [specific mention of what this company offers]."

Avoid:

  • "In 5 years I want your job" — presumptuous
  • "I want to be a manager" — unless they're hiring for that path
  • "I'm not sure" — signals lack of direction

followUps.question: What are your short-term career goals?
followUps.answer:
Short-term means 1-2 years. Be specific and achievable — this shouldn't sound like a wishlist.

Good structure:

"In the next 12-18 months, I have three specific goals:

1. Technical depth: I want to get much stronger in [system design / cloud infrastructure / distributed systems]. Right now I understand the concepts but I want production experience designing systems that handle real scale under real constraints.

2. Contribution scope: I want to move from 'implementing well-defined requirements' to 'helping shape what gets built.' I'd like to be in design discussions early, writing technical proposals, and having a seat at the table where architectural decisions are made.

3. Certification/credentials: I'm working toward [AZ-204 Azure Developer / AWS SAA / relevant certification] to formalize my cloud knowledge. Not because I think a cert is the goal, but because studying for it forces me to close knowledge gaps systematically."

Tie at least one goal back to what this role offers. It shows this isn't a placeholder.

followUps.question: What skills are you actively developing?
followUps.answer:
Be specific and honest. The worst answer is a list of buzzwords you're not actually working on.

Good answer:

"Right now I'm actively working on three things:

  • System design: I've been studying distributed systems patterns — consistent hashing, leader election, event-driven architecture. I worked through the 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' book and I'm applying the concepts in a side project where I'm building a simplified message queue.
  • Cloud infrastructure: I've been deepening my Azure knowledge specifically around AKS, Azure Service Bus, and infrastructure-as-code with Bicep/Terraform. I've spun up some experimental workloads to understand how the pieces fit together in production.
  • Communication: I'm working on explaining technical decisions more clearly to non-technical stakeholders. I've been writing more internal documentation and design docs as practice — getting feedback on clarity and conciseness.

I try to have one 'deep skill' I'm building (system design right now), one 'breadth skill' (cloud/DevOps), and one soft skill. Rotating through only tech skills leaves gaps."

questions.tip: Tie your 5-year goal to this role. Show that this job is a step toward your goal, not just a placeholder. This tells the interviewer you've thought about your career and that this role genuinely serves it.

questions.question: What is your greatest strength?
questions.answer:
Pick a strength that is: real (you can prove it), relevant to the role, and specific (not generic).

Bad answer: "I'm a hard worker and a good team player." (Everyone says this.)

Good answer structure:

"One of my strongest skills is [specific technical/problem-solving skill]. For example, in [project], I was able to [specific situation]. I approached it by [what you did], which resulted in [specific outcome].

More broadly, I'm good at [meta-skill — breaking down complex problems / communicating technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders / picking up new technologies quickly] and that's been valuable in projects like [brief reference]."

Strengths relevant for .NET developers:

  • Debugging and root-cause analysis
  • Translating business requirements into clean technical designs
  • Picking up new technologies quickly (Angular, Azure, etc.)
  • Writing maintainable, well-structured code
  • Performance optimization instincts

followUps.question: Can you give me a specific example of that strength?
followUps.answer:
This is a STAR follow-up. Have one concrete story ready for whatever strength you claim. The story should be specific, verifiable, and ideally have a measurable or observable outcome.

Example (strength: debugging / root-cause analysis):

"In the CSInvoice project, we had a production bug where invoices were occasionally being saved with a subtotal that didn't match the line items. The inconsistency was subtle — it happened maybe 1 in 200 saves, and only under specific timing conditions.

I used structured query logging (EF Core query log + Serilog) to capture the exact SQL being executed during a bad save. I discovered that the subtotal was being calculated client-side in JavaScript and sent to the API, and under a specific race condition in the form's reactive state, the total was being computed before the last line item was added. The fix was straightforward — move the calculation server-side so the API always recomputes it from the submitted line items. But finding it took 2 days of careful log analysis and reproducing it in a test harness.

The strength that made the difference there wasn't knowing the answer — it was being systematic: adding instrumentation first, hypothesizing a cause, designing a reproduction case, and not guessing."

followUps.question: What's your greatest weakness?
followUps.answer:
Pick a real weakness you're genuinely working to improve — not a disguised strength. Interviewers see through "I work too hard" or "I'm a perfectionist."

Good weakness format: Name the weakness → give an honest example of when it showed up → describe what you're actively doing to improve it.

Example answer:

"One weakness I'm actively working on is knowing when to stop optimizing and ship. I tend to want the code to be elegant and efficient, and sometimes I'll spend extra time refactoring or tuning performance before showing it to the team — even when 'good enough' was the right call for the current stage.

I noticed this was slowing down my PRs in a few cases where I spent two days optimizing something that would realistically handle the load fine for the next year. I've been working on it by timeboxing refactoring efforts: I give myself a set amount of time to improve something, and when the time's up, I ship it with a note about what I'd improve next iteration if it became a bottleneck. It's helped me ship faster without feeling like I'm compromising quality."

Key: The interviewer is testing self-awareness and growth mindset — not looking for disqualifying flaws.

questions.tip: Greatest weakness follow-up: Pick a real weakness you're actively working on — not a disguised strength ("I work too hard"). Example: "I sometimes spend too much time optimizing code before shipping. I've been working on recognizing when 'good enough' is the right call for the current stage."

questions.question: How do you handle tight deadlines or pressure?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know you don't panic, you communicate, and you make smart tradeoffs.

Good answer:

"When I'm under a tight deadline, the first thing I do is prioritize ruthlessly — what absolutely must ship vs what's nice-to-have. I've learned that trying to do everything at 80% quality is worse than doing the critical parts at 100% and deferring the rest.

I also communicate early. If I see a deadline is at risk, I tell the team or manager as soon as I'm aware — not the day before. Early warning gives us options (scope reduction, extra help, deadline extension). Silence until the last minute doesn't help anyone.

In [specific project], we had a tight deadline where [situation]. I [specific action — broke it down, cut scope, worked with the team]. We delivered [result — what shipped, what was deferred and why that was the right call].

The one thing I try to avoid under pressure is cutting corners on code quality in ways that create tech debt that takes 3x longer to fix later. I'd rather have a productive conversation about scope than ship something fragile."

followUps.question: Tell me about a time you missed a deadline.
followUps.answer:
Own it. The interviewer is testing accountability and learning — not perfection.

What NOT to do: Blame the requirements, the client, a colleague, or circumstances. Even if those were factors, leading with blame is a red flag.

Good structure:

"Yes, I've missed a deadline. In [project], we committed to delivering [feature] by [date]. About a week before the deadline, I realized the scope was larger than estimated — specifically, [the third-party integration had undocumented edge cases / the data migration was more complex than anticipated].

I informed [manager/team] immediately rather than hoping I'd close the gap. We had a conversation about options: cut scope (remove [non-critical part]), extend the deadline by [X days], or bring in extra help. We chose to [option], delivered [what actually shipped], and the remaining [piece] was shipped [X] days later.

What I learned: my estimation was overconfident because I hadn't built in buffer for the unknowns in a third-party integration. I now build in a 20-30% estimation buffer for any work that depends on external systems or undocumented APIs, and I do a spike/prototype before committing to a firm delivery date for high-uncertainty work."

questions.tip: If you missed a deadline: Own it, explain what happened, what you learned, and what you'd do differently. Don't blame external factors. Accountability and learning from mistakes is valued more than perfection.

questions.question: Why do you want to work here?
questions.answer:
This question tests: Did you research us? Do you actually want this job or are we just option #12?

Answer formula: Company-specific reason + Role-specific fit + Your goal alignment

Research angles:

  • Tech stack — "I've been using .NET and SQL Server for 5 years, and I see your team is building on these. I can contribute immediately while also learning [their specific tech]."
  • Product — "I've been following [company's product] for a while. The [specific feature/challenge] is technically interesting to me because [genuine reason]."
  • Team/culture — "The engineering blog post about [specific topic] showed how seriously you take [code quality / engineering culture / specific practice]. That's the kind of team I want to work with."
  • Scale/challenge — "The scale at which you operate [millions of users, high throughput] is the kind of engineering challenge I want to work on next."

What to avoid:

  • "You pay well" (even if true)
  • "It's close to my home"
  • Generic "great company culture" without specifics

followUps.question: What do you know about our product/company?
followUps.answer:
This is a test of whether you did the 30-minute homework before the interview. If you haven't researched the company, you will fail this question. There's no way to improvise a convincing answer.

What to research before any interview:

  • Product: What does the company actually make/sell? Who are the customers? What problem does it solve? What's the pricing model?
  • Tech stack: LinkedIn job postings, engineering blog, StackShare, GitHub repos if public
  • Recent news: Funding rounds, product launches, acquisitions, company blog
  • Engineering culture: Engineering blog posts, tech talks on YouTube, Glassdoor engineering reviews
  • Competitors: Who else is in the space? How is this company differentiated?

Example answer: "From what I've seen, [Company] is building [product description]. I read your engineering blog post about [specific post] — the way you handled [specific technical challenge] was genuinely interesting, specifically [the part about X]. I also saw you recently [launched feature / raised series B / open-sourced Y]. It seems like the team is focused on [growth / technical excellence / shipping fast] and that's the kind of environment I want to be in."

followUps.question: What makes you a better fit than other candidates?
followUps.answer:
This is a confidence and differentiation question. Don't be falsely modest ("I don't know who else applied") and don't be arrogant ("I'm the best developer you'll find"). Be direct and specific.

Good structure:

"I can't speak to other candidates, but I can tell you what I think I bring that's particularly relevant to this role:

1. Full-stack .NET depth: I work across the whole stack — API design, EF Core data layer, Angular frontend, CI/CD pipelines. I don't hand off work at the boundary between layers; I understand how they fit together and can debug issues that span them.

2. Relevant project experience: I've [built background services in production / designed APIs used by external clients / worked with the specific domain you're in]. That's not just theoretical knowledge — I've hit the real edge cases.

3. Fast ramp-up: Your stack is [tech X, Y, Z]. I've worked extensively with [X and Y]. I've done enough with [Z] to be productive quickly and I'm comfortable learning in context rather than needing everything handed to me.

Beyond the skills, I'm specifically interested in this company — not just any .NET role. That motivation translates to engagement."

questions.tip: Spend 30 minutes researching before every interview. LinkedIn, company engineering blog, Glassdoor, recent news. Mention something specific you found. It takes candidates from forgettable to memorable.

questions.question: How do you keep yourself updated with new .NET technologies?
questions.answer:
This tests whether you're passionate about the craft and proactive about growth.

Genuine sources I follow:

  • .NET Blog (dotnet.microsoft.com/blog) — official announcements and deep dives
  • Scott Hanselman's blog — practical .NET tips from inside Microsoft
  • YouTube: Nick Chapsas (clean architecture, new features), Tim Corey, Raw Coding
  • Microsoft Learn / docs.microsoft.com — official documentation for new APIs
  • GitHub: Follow .NET runtime repo, ASP.NET Core repo — see what's coming in preview
  • Dev.to, Medium — community articles about real-world usage

Active learning methods:

  • Build small side projects with new features (that's how I tried out Minimal APIs and .NET 8's primary constructors)
  • Contribute to personal projects to experiment with new patterns
  • Read source code of popular libraries (I've learned a lot reading Polly's and EF Core's internals)

"The .NET release cycle (one major version per year) gives a natural rhythm — I always spend time with the What's New page for each release and try the highlighted features in a sandbox project."

followUps.question: What's a recent .NET feature you're excited about?
followUps.answer:
Name something specific. Have an opinion — not just "it's a useful feature" but why it matters to you specifically.

Good examples to choose from:

  • C# 12 Primary Constructors: "I've been using primary constructors extensively. They reduce the boilerplate for DI — no more six-line constructor bodies just to assign fields. The code is noticeably more readable, especially in services with 3-4 injected dependencies."
  • .NET 8 Native AOT: "AOT compilation is a meaningful shift for .NET in the cloud. Startup time from ~200ms to under 10ms and a fraction of the memory — that changes the economics of serverless .NET. I've been experimenting with it for Azure Functions use cases."
  • .NET Aspire: "The orchestration for local microservices development is something the .NET ecosystem needed. Being able to define your services, databases, and message brokers in code and have them wire up locally with health checks and dashboards changes how you develop distributed systems."
  • System.Text.Json incremental improvements: "The source generator support and the new JsonSerializer options in .NET 8 have made it competitive with Newtonsoft in all the cases where I used to reach for it."

followUps.question: What would you build if you had unlimited time?
followUps.answer:
This question reveals your genuine interests and technical ambitions. There's no wrong answer — be authentic and a little enthusiastic. It's also a chance to show that you think about real problems, not just tech for its own sake.

Example answer:

"Honestly, I'd build a personal finance tool that actually understands your spending patterns in context — not just categorization, but something that can reason about your goals and flag when your spending trajectory doesn't match them. The existing tools (Mint, YNAB) are great at tracking, but weak at insight and recommendation.

Technically, I'd build it as an event-sourced system — every transaction is an immutable event, and your current 'state' is always a projection. This gives you perfect audit history and the ability to recompute any aggregation at any point in time. I'd use .NET for the API, SQLite/Event Store for persistence, and an LLM-powered analysis layer via Azure OpenAI for the natural language insight engine.

I'm interested in this problem partly because I've been working with financial domain models in [CSInvoice/FolioTrack] and I see how much complexity lives in correctly modeling money, rounding, and multi-currency — it's technically interesting despite looking simple."

Tailor the project to your genuine interests. If you'd build a game, an open-source tool, or a developer productivity tool — say that. Authenticity reads better than a calculated answer.

questions.tip: Name a specific recent .NET feature — .NET 9 AOT, C# 12 primary constructors, interceptors, .NET Aspire, Blazor SSR, etc. Having a concrete example shows you actually follow the ecosystem, not just claim to.
